retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Sarcoidosis -- Treatment / Management -- Biologic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: These are used in cases of refractory non-infectious uveitis. The most commonly used biologic agents are tumor necrosis factor-alpha inhibitors (TNF α) such as infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, and golimumab. These agents promise to offer a new option in resistant cases. The patient should be tested for latent tuberculosis and hepatitis B before starting biologic agents to prevent the reactivation of infection. [109] Interestingly, a 'sarcoid-like granulomatosis' has been reported following anti-TNF therapy, including etanercept, infliximab, and adalimumab for various indications, including rheumatoid arthritis and spondyloarthritis. [110]

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoidosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Symptomatic patients need drug therapy, and the pharmacist needs to emphasize the importance of compliance and the need to follow up since most drugs have adverse side effects. Patients who fail to respond to steroids may need potent biological agents. In addition, patients with end-stage lung disease may need to be seen by a transplant nurse to determine their eligibility. Further, patients with eye symptoms should be referred to an ophthalmologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of golimumab. Evaluate the potential adverse effects of golimumab. Determine the appropriate monitoring parameters for patients receiving golimumab. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members and patients to improve care coordination, optimize golimumab therapy for treating inflammatory conditions, and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Sarcoidosis -- Treatment / Management -- Immunosuppressive Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid-sparing agents are regularly used in conjunction with corticosteroids to minimize steroid exposure. While no consensus guidelines exist, many clinicians use methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil with steroids or in patients who appear to be steroid-resistant. Other therapeutic agents used include azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, and leflunomide. [5] Immunologic therapies such as infliximab, adalimumab, and rituximab are considered third- or fourth-line agents in treating CS because of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoidosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lung transplant is an option for patients with end-stage lung disease. However, the transplant is also associated with risks and the need to take immunosuppressive therapy for life.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects, including severe infections and invasive fungal infections, may occur. Treatment should not be initiated during any active infections. Patients with hepatitis B are at risk for reactivation. Administration of golimumab may lead to congestive heart failure (CHF) or exacerbate underlying CHF symptoms. Lupus-like syndrome and hypersensitivity reactions such as anaphylaxis may also occur. Worsening or new-onset demyelinating disorders have also been reported. [17] Common adverse effects associated with golimumab administration include:

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoidosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: At each clinic visit, the nurse should ensure that the patient gets a regular 12-lead ECG because the heart block is not uncommon. The pharmacist and nurse should educate the patient on the importance of discontinuing smoking and abstaining from alcohol. Finally, patients who are managed with steroids should be educated by all members of the team about the side effects of the drugs used. Only through such an interprofessional team approach can the outcomes of sarcoidosis be improved. [14] [15] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals benefit from updated knowledge regarding golimumab administration, making informed decisions, and optimizing dosage regimens to reduce adverse reactions. This program improves patient outcomes by providing healthcare professionals with evidence-based guidance on treating RA, PA, UC, and pcJIA with golimumab. This activity provides healthcare professionals with information regarding the latest therapeutic advances, allowing for better management of these challenging conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Sarcoidosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical course shows variability. Few patients may have acute self-limited disease, while others experience chronic, progressive deterioration. Corticosteroids remain the mainstay of treatment with the newer addition of immunosuppressants and biologics in the treatment armamentarium.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: The product label does not include specific dosage adjustments for patients with hepatic impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Testing for active or latent tuberculosis (TB) (ie, tuberculin skin test (TST) or interferon-γ release assay (IGRA)) should occur before initiating golimumab therapy. Patients should also be routinely monitored during TB treatment after starting golimumab therapy. Patients with latent TB should be treated before initiating golimumab. Patients should also receive a baseline complete blood count (CBC) and routinely afterward. Patients with an active infection should not be started on golimumab. Monitoring for bacterial, viral, fungal, or opportunistic infections while on treatment is also advised. The Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score (ASDAS) is useful for monitoring response to therapy. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: No labeled contraindications have been reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoidosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary sarcoidosis is often an asymptomatic, non-progressive disease and requires no treatment, as a majority of patients undergo spontaneous remission. Close monitoring of symptoms, chest radiograph, and pulmonary function is continued at three to six-month intervals should be considered in asymptomatic patients. For patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis causing worsening symptoms, stage II to III radiographic findings should be considered for oral glucocorticoids at 0.3 to 0.6 mg/kg for 4 to 6 weeks. If there is no improvement in symptoms, radiographic abnormalities, and pulmonary function tests, steroids may be continued for an additional four to six weeks. Maintainance steroids are not needed; steroid tapering to a dose of 0.25 to 0.5 mg/kg (usually 10 to 20 mg) per day should be considered over at least six to eight months. Methotrexate, azathioprine, infliximab, leflunomide, and antimalarial agents may be considered as steroid-sparing agents in patients who are unable to tolerate steroids. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinitis -- Treatment / Management -- Sarcoidosis
retrieved_docs.content: The initial management of the ocular symptoms of sarcoidosis involves systemic oral or intravenous glucocorticoids in doses of 1 to 1.5 mg/kg/d. Methotrexate and mycophenolate mofetil are often utilized early in ocular disease as steroid-sparing agents. Azathioprine and cyclosporine are additional alternatives. Clinicians may consider adalimumab and other systemic immunomodulators if other immunosuppressive medications fail.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treating patients with inflammatory and autoimmune conditions requires coordination from an interprofessional healthcare team. Immediate clinical examination and diagnosis can enhance the efficiency of treatment plans, mitigating debilitating symptoms and increasing the quality of the patient's life. The interprofessional treatment team comprises a primary care physician (PCP), a rheumatologist, a gastroenterologist, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, nurses, a physical therapist (PT), and a pharmacist. Pharmacists should verify dosing, perform drug interaction checks, and report discrepancies to the prescriber. Nurses should monitor patients for adherence and clinical response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids represent a class of corticosteroid medications that includes agents such as prednison and paramethason. Both prednison and paramethason are synthetic glucocorticoid drugs administered systemically to exert potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By belonging to the systemic glucocorticoid category, these agents share common mechanisms of action, pharmacologic properties, and therapeutic indications, making them useful in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Understanding that prednison and paramethason are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and provide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, prednison, rimexolone, and paramethason are all members of the drug class identified as Glucocorticoids, Systemic. As systemic glucocorticoids, these agents share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are used in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Recognizing that each of these medications belongs to the same systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and therapeutic indications, facilitating appropriate selection and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon is identified as a therapeutic option for treating التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis), but its use is contraindicated in patients who have حالةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (a fibrotic condition). In contrast, hydrocortisone sodium succinate is indicated for the management of حالةٌ رَبْوِيَّة, providing a treatment alternative for fibrotic disorders, and it is also employed in the treatment of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). These relationships highlight the distinct therapeutic roles and safety considerations of triamcinolon and hydrocortisone sodium succinate in different clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason, a potent glucocorticoid, is commonly employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), where it helps to diminish inflammation and improve the characteristic skin lesions of the disease. This therapeutic link highlights the important role of corticosteroid agents such as betamethason in treating inflammatory dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone sodium phosphate and methylprednisolone sodium succinate are both corticosteroid formulations that may be employed in the treatment of اضطراب الدم (blood disorder). These agents share a similar therapeutic role, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help manage the underlying pathophysiology of various hematologic abnormalities. Utilizing either formulation provides clinicians with options for addressing blood‑related disorders within patient‑centered care plans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1471
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) may be treated with triamcinolon, a drug that belongs to the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids, represented by both triamcinolon and rimexolone, share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are employed in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Recognizing that triamcinolon and rimexolone are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group informs clinicians in selecting appropriate steroid therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid with anti‑inflammatory properties, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), highlighting its role in managing inflammatory and autoimmune neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
retrieved_docs.content: Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1748
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used to attenuate immune system activity. Members of this class comprise inhibitory interleukin, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, daklizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochloride of mykofenolát mofetilu, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. Each of these compounds functions to suppress immune responses and is employed in clinical settings such as organ transplantation, treatment of autoimmune disorders, and management of inflammatory conditions, highlighting the diverse therapeutic options available within the immunosuppressant category.
